questions.microTopic: Agriculture (Schemes, APMC, MSP, Exports, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Grassland ecosystem
II. 	 Parasitic ecosystem
III. 	Pond ecosystem
IV. 	 Forest ecosystem
V. 	 Tree ecosystem
How many of the above exhibit an upright pyramid
of numbers?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **pyramid of numbers** shows the total number of individual organisms per unit area at each trophic level in the food chain of an ecosystem. In this, individuals at each successive trophic level are counted and their numbers are plotted in the form of pyramids. The shape of the pyramid of numbers may vary from one ecosystem to another ecosystem.
● **In grassland ecosystems the pyramid of numbers is upright.** The producers in the grassland are the grasses which are small in size and large in number per unit area. So the producers form a broad base in the pyramid. The herbivores in the grassland are the insects; carnivores are frogs, birds, etc. and top carnivores are hawk, eagle, foxes etc. which are gradually less and less in number and so the pyramid apex becomes gradually narrower forming an upright and erect pyramid. **So, point I is correct.**
● For a **parasitic ecosystem,** the primary producers (host) are lesser (or even singular) in number, on
which multiple organisms are parasites such as fleas, lice, leaches etc. These primary parasites further harbors other parasites or are parasited upon by numerous microscopic organisms like trichonympha etc. These organisms are often suprainfected by viruses such as bacteriophages for bacteria etc.
which are further numerous in number. **Hence, the** **pyramid of number in a parasitic ecosystem is** **inverted in nature. So, point II is not correct.**
● **In a forest ecosystem,** large sized trees are the producers, which are less in number and so form a narrow base**.** The trees support a large number of herbivores like insects, birds, frogs, etc. and hence form a middle broad level. The secondary consumers like predatory birds (hawks, eagles, etc.), foxes, snakes, lizards, etc. are less in number than herbivores. **So the** **pyramid assumes a spindle shape with** **narrow on both ends and broader in the** **middle**. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **In a pond ecosystem the pyramid of** **numbers is always upright,** the base is formed by the primary producers phytoplanktons, followed by primary consumers zooplanktons, which are fed upon by smaller fishes, then comes the tertiary consumers large fishes like scoliodon. **So, point III is correct.**
● **A** **tree ecosystem has an inverted** **pyramid** **of** **numbers.** The number of producers in the tree ecosystem is the smallest, and the population of consumers grows at each trophic level**. So, point V is** **not correct.**
**So, only two of the above ecosystems** **exhibit** **an** **upright** **pyramid** **of** **numbers.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct** **answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The amount of energy transmitted from one
trophic level to the next decreases with each
successive trophic level.
**Statement II:**
The transfer of energy in an ecosystem is governed
by the Second Law of Thermodynamics.
**Statements III:**
Unlike the flow of nutrients, the flow of energy in
an ecosystem is always unidirectional.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the statements given above?

options.a:
Both Statement-II and Statement-III are
correct and both of them explain Statement-I
options.b:
Both Statement-II and Statement-III are
correct, but only one of them explains
Statement-I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements-II and III is
correct, and that explains Statement-I
options.d:
Neither Statement-II nor Statement-III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Energy is transferred between trophic levels in the form of food when one organism eats another in** **the ecosystem.** There is approximately 80- 90% loss of energy at each transfer. When energy enters a trophic level, some of it is stored as biomass, as part of the organism's body. This is the energy available to the next trophic level since only energy stored as biomass can get eaten. As a rule of thumb, **only about** **10 percent of energy available at one trophic level is passed on to the next. So, statement I is correct.**
● For ecosystems to be self-sustaining, lower trophic levels should have more energy than the higher trophic levels. This helps the organisms at lower levels to maintain a stable population, but also to transfer energy up the pyramid.
● The **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, also known as the **law of entropy**, states that randomness of energy increases over time, that is as the reaction or processes continue energy is converted from a concentrated form to a more random form. In simpler terms, it states that **as energy is transferred, more** **and more of it is wasted. It thus expresses that usable energy consistently diminishes.**
● **By this Law, energy flow declines from producer level to successive trophic levels.** When energy is transferred to next trophic levels, only about 10% of it is utilized to assemble body mass and become stored energy. The remaining 90% is lost in metabolic activities. **So, statement II is correct.**
● In ecosystems**, there is a unidirectional movement of energy** towards the higher trophic levels. It flows in one direction through the food chain, from producers to consumers, and is not recycled back. Once energy is used by an organism, it is typically lost as heat and is not available for reuse by other organisms at lower trophic levels. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, both statement II and statement III are correct and both of them explain statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The pyramid of energy represents the amount of energy present at each trophic level. It starts with the producers and ends with consumers at higher trophic levels. It is also called the pyramid of productivity. Since the productivity or energy flow is expressed per unit time basis, the pyramid is always in an upright position.
● Pyramids of energy are always upright since energy decreases at each trophic level. It can never be inverted because when energy flows from a particular trophic level to the next trophic level, some energy is always lost as heat at each step.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Long tap root system
II. 	 High cell osmotic pressure
III. 	Subterranean habitat
IV. 	 Short life cycle
The above adaptations are most likely to be seen in
the plants/animals of which one of the following
ecosystems?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Desert ecosystems** are located in regions that receive low precipitation, generally less than 25 cm per year. They occupy about 17 percent of land on earth. Due to the extremely high temperature, low water availability and intense sunlight, vegetation is scarce or poorly developed. They are highly adapted to the dry conditions.The vegetation is mainly shrubs, bushes, few grasses and rare trees.
● **Plants growing in dry desert habitats** are referred to as xerophytes. These plants have certain morphological and physiological features that enable them to survive in dry habitats.
○ They have few or no leaves which reduce transpiration ○ The leaf blades, pinnae and pinnules of xerophytic plants are smaller in size and more compact. ○ The plant species have an extensive root system. The plants possess a **long tap root system that** **penetrates deep into the soil**. The extensive root system also helps the soil in retaining water. The production of an extensive root system increases the absorptive capacity of the plant but reduces the exposure of the plant to the atmosphere. ○ **Desert plants become succulents.** Succulents are plants in which organs become smaller and fleshy due to accumulation of water. The bulk of the plant body is composed of water storing tissues. Water stored in these tissues is used during the period of water scarcity. ○ In many xerophytes, the epidermis is multilayered, heavily cutinized and waxy to provide greater resistance to desiccation under extreme dry conditions. ○ The **cells possess high osmotic pressure** and a very high water-potential. This is because of the high solute content of the soil. This prevents cellular collapse under water-stress conditions. ○ **Some plants in hot deserts are ephemeral**, meaning they have a very short life cycle or a very short period of active growth.
■ Ephemeralism, the characteristic of having a very short life cycle, is a key adaptation in desert ecosystems, allowing plants to quickly complete their life cycle during brief periods of rainfall. These plants, also known as drought evaders or drought escapers, survive dry periods as dormant seeds, and upon rainfall, rapidly germinate, flower, and reproduce before the soil dries out again
● **Animals adapt to arid desert conditions** in the following way: ○ They conserve water by excreting concentrated urine ○ Animals are nocturnal in habit to avoid the sun’s heat during day time ○ Animals usually have long legs to keep the body away from the hot ground ○ **Animals live in burrows and other underground spaces to escape sunlight (A subterranean** **environment refers to underground spaces)** ○ Animals have scaly skin, resistant to drying **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Species Meaning
I. - Keystone Species Most abundant species
II. - Indicator Species Indicator of environmental changes
III. - Flagship Species Species with a large
geographical range
IV. - Umbrella Species Species whose conservation leads to protection of other co-habitating species
Which of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Keystone Species** is a species whose loss from an ecosystem would cause a greater than average change in other species populations or ecosystems processes. These are **species that have a disproportionately** **large effect on other species in a community.**
○ Keystone species **may be relatively rare in natural communities** or may not be easily recognized. **They are not always the largest or most abundant species in an ecosystem. So,** **pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **An indicator species** is a species or group of species chosen as an indicator of, o**r proxy for, the state of** **an ecosystem or of a certain process within that ecosystem.** An indicator species describes an organism that is very sensitive to environmental changes in its ecosystem.
○ **Indicator species are very sensitive to environmental changes and almost immediately** **affected by changes to the ecosystem** which gives early warning that a habitat is suffering. Examples include crayfish as indicators of freshwater quality, and Lichens are indicators of air quality. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **A Flagship Species is a species selected to act as an ambassador, icon or symbol** for a defined habitat, issue, campaign or environmental cause. They can be mascots for entire ecosystems. The identification of a flagship species relies heavily on the social, cultural, and economic value of a species. **So, pair III is** **not correctly matched.**
○ Flagship species may or may not be keystone species or indicator species and may or may not be good indicators of biological process.
● **An Umbrella Species is defined as a species whose conservation indirectly protects other species that** **share its habitat.** Umbrella species have large habitat needs, and the requirements of that habitat impact many other species living there. Giant panda is an excellent example of an umbrella species conferring protection on many other species where pandas live. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
Fires are important for the maintenance of natural
grassland ecosystems.
**Statement II:**
Fires suppress the growth of woody trees.
**Statements III:**
Fires reduce the leaf litter and also warms the soil.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the statements given above?

options.a:
Both Statement -II and Statement -III are
correct and both of them explain Statement -I
options.b:
Both Statement -II and Statement -III are
correct, but only one of them explains
Statement -I
options.c:
Only one of the Statement-II and III is correct,
and that explains Statement-I
options.d:
Neither Statement-II nor Statement-III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Fire is an important tool for restoring and preserving natural grasslands, which are essential to the survival of thousands of species. After a fire, blackened fields quickly come back to life with new, green grasses. This **role of fire is more prominent in the grassland ecosystem, which depends on routine fires to** **thrive. So, statement I is correct.**
● The **pertinent requirement of a grassland to form and thrive is to stop the woody plants from** **growing**, these woody plants can eliminate the soft grasses by competing with them for space and nutrients. The **woody plants act as a fodder for sporadic fire episodes**, further these woody plants take more time to grow while the grasses regenerate at a faster pace. After a fire, grazing animals are attracted to the lush new growth in a burned area, helping to prevent new woody plants from returning.  **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● Fire helps maintain grassland health and vigor. It **reduces the leaf litter that accumulates each year,** **allowing sunlight to penetrate and stimulate new growth**. Fire **also warms the soil**, increasing microbial activity, which releases nutrients from decaying plant material that new grasses and flowers need to grow**. So, statement III is correct.** **So, both statement -II and statement -III are correct and both of them explain statement -I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following Ramsar sites is famous
for floating islands called ‘Phumdi,’ and is home
to the world's only floating national park?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Loktak Lake is a Ramsar Site in Manipur that is famous for a unique ecosystem called** **‘Phumdi’,** the floating mats of soil and vegetation. The Phumdi floats during the rains and sinks during dry months.
● The largest area of the Phumdi in the Loktak lake is in the **Keibul Lamjao National Park,** which is home to endangered brow-antlered deer, the **Sangai**.
● **Keibul Lamjao National Park is the only floating national park in the world.** Loktak Lake is the largest freshwater lake in northeast India**.**
● **Sangai** is one of the three sub-species of Eld’s deer. Sangai, also called **the brow-antlered deer,** is found only in the northeastern state of Manipur. The animal is a medium-sized deer, with uniquely distinctive antlers, measuring 100 -110 cm in length.
○ It **lives in the marshy wetland of the Keibul Lamjao National Park (KLNP),** the only floating park in the world. The KLNP is an integral part of the Loktak Lake and is characterized by many floating decomposed plant materials locally called phumdis. ○ It is listed as **‘Endangered’ in IUCN Red List** and Schedule I of the Indian WildLife (Protection) Act, 1972. Sangai is also called **the dancing deer.** Sangai is the **state animal of Manipur.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Renuka Wetland** is a natural wetland with freshwater springs and inland subterranean karst formations. It is located in Himachal Pradesh. It is a Ramsar Site.
● **Deepor Beel** is a freshwater lake in Assam. It is in a former channel of the Brahmaputra river. The beel is a staging site on migratory flyways and some of the largest concentrations of aquatic birds in Assam can be seen, especially in winter. It is a Ramsar Site.
● **Chilika Lake** is a brackish water lake and a shallow lagoon in the state of Odisha in eastern India. Chilika Lake is the largest brackish water lake with estuarine character in India. It is the largest wintering ground for migratory birds in India. Chilika lake has been designated as a "Ramsar Site." The Nalaban Island within the lake is notified as a Bird Sanctuary under Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Aquatic Ecosystem and Life (Aquatic Ecosystem, Maritime Zones, Corals, Mangrove, Marine Vegetation)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following organisms are most
likely to undergo the process of co-extinction,
which is if any one of the organisms becomes
extinct the other will inadvertently become
extinct?

options.d: Fig tree and its pollinating wasp

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Co-extinctions** refer to the events when a species becomes extinct**, the plant and animal species** **associated with it** in an obligatory way also become extinct. When a host fish species becomes extinct, its unique assemblage of parasites also meets the same fate. It is one of the four major factors of biodiversity loss comprising the **Evil Quatret**.
● **Plant and its pollinating agent** are a characteristic example of mutualism, with both being an essential component of each other’s life cycle, often these species are co-evolved in an ecosystem. Extinction of one can inadvertently lead to the extinction of other species as well.
● The **fig plant** has male and female flowers. When the latter are ready, the fig emits an aroma to attract the female of its specific wasp species looking to lay eggs. When she enters through a miniscule opening, her wings and antennae break off. Hence, she can’t leave. As she lays her eggs on many flowers, she also spreads the pollen from the fig in which she was born. When the eggs hatch, the males are born without wings. Their only purpose is to mate with the females born along with them. Once this is done, they die within the fig.
● **When the females are ready to leave, the male flowers are loaded with pollen. So, the wasps leave** **with a supply of pollen and pollinate the female flowers of the fig in which she will lay her eggs**. Since fig flowers bloom internally, they cannot rely on the wind or other insects to spread their pollen. In a textbook case of mutualism, **the fig cannot survive without the fig wasp and vice-versa.**
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Chital and Cheetah**: Cheetah being the top predator in an ecosystem, prefers primary herbivorous organisms like Chitals for their feast, but these two are not in any way in an obligatory relationship, because Cheetah have a broad food base, in absence of Chitals it can easily diversify its prey base.
● **Sea Anemones and Coral Reefs:** Corals are surface dwelling while anemones are bottom dwelling, no such direct association is seen in these two groups.
● **Sucker fish and Shark:** Sucker fish is a commensal on shark, it feeds on the leftover food left by the latter. In case of Sucker fish extinction shark will have no change in its lifestyle.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following ecosystems in the increasing
order of their rate of productivity:
I. 	 Coral reefs
II. 	 Estuarine areas
III. 	Open oceans
IV. 	 Temperate grasslands
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Plant productivity is measured as the **rate at which biomass/ energy is produced** per unit area/ unit time. Productivity is categorised into:
o **Gross Primary Productivity (GPP):** It is the total organic matter produced by photosynthesis. Plants use a portion of this for respiration. GPP is the rate at which plants fix carbon by capturing radiant solar energy to produce carbohydrates. o **Net Primary Productivity (NPP):** The remaining organic matter after subtracting respiration from GPP, is available for consumption by heterotrophs (herbivores and decomposers) and is called NPP. o **Secondary Productivity**: The rate at which consumers form new organic matter.
● **Coral reefs** are among the **most productive** **ecosystems**, with the primary producers at the base of the food chain (including corals) supporting their complex food webs.
● **Estuarine areas:** Large scale availability of nutrients brought in by the flowing rivers make this ecosystem highly productive.
● **Open oceans:** Despite being abundant for water and sunlight, this ecosystem is highly deficient in nutrient supply, thus making it one of the least productive ecosystems on the Earth.
● **Temperate grasslands:** These are considered as an ecotone between the desert and forest ecosystem, therefore their rate of productivity lies between that of forest and desert ecosystem. Being more than that of desert but less than forest. **So, the correct increasing order of given ecosystems based on their rate of productivity is: Open Oceans –** **Temperate Grasslands – Estuarine areas – Coral reefs.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Ecosystem productivity is determined by many factors and can be very spatial and temporal in nature.**
● **Solar Energy:** Tropical regions with abundant sunlight throughout the year are likely to have higher productivity as compared to polar or temperate regions with limited sunlight during certain seasons.
● **Availability of Nutrients:** Ecosystems with copious amounts of nutrients, such as estuaries and wetlands, tend to have high productivity. Those with low nutrient levels are open oceans or deserts, and productivity is low in these regions.
● **Water Availability:** Land systems with low water like in arid, semi-arid, and hot desert regions, such as deserts and grasslands, tend to have lower productivity than humid systems, like tropical rainforests.
● **Temperature:** It influences both plants and animals' metabolic activities. The rate of productivity is enhanced when it is warm by accelerating photosynthesis and growth rates. Tundra-type cold conditions lead to low productivity because the sun energy is minimal, and biological processes are slow.
● **Carbon Dioxide (CO****2****):** It is an important input in photosynthesis. Increased CO2 can enhance primary production, in an effect known as CO2 fertilization, but this potentially may be constrained by other factors including nutrients.
● **Disruptions:** Natural disturbances (such as fires, floods and hurricanes) and human activities (including deforestation and pollution) can have a very considerable impact on productivity in the ecosystem. Some of these disturbances can actually recycle nutrients and rejuvenate the ecosystem, while others can result in degradation and decreased productivity.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Decomposition is largely an anaerobic
process.
II. 	 Animal wastes are decomposed at a faster
rate than plant wastes.
III.	 During decomposition, heat is released into
the atmosphere.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Decomposition** is the **breakdown of dead matter,** which is often called rotting. **Decomposition is largely** **an oxygen-requiring process**, with aerobic organisms predominantly taking part in the process of decomposition. It must be noted here that sufficient aeration can accentuate the process of decomposition, this is the underlying principle of waste management processes like biosparging, slurping etc. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● **The rate of decomposition is controlled by chemical composition of detritus and climatic factors.** In a particular climatic condition. decomposition rate is slower if detritus is rich in lignin and chitin, and quicker, if detritus is rich in nitrogen and water -soluble substances like sugars. **As the plant cell wall is composed of** **complex polymers like lignin, cellulose and chitin,** it is decomposed more slowly than the animal wastes. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Decomposition is an exothermic process**, that is it is a process where **heat is released into the atmosphere.** Any chemical reaction where the chemical bonds are broken down involves release of energy as the energy stored in the bonds get released. Also when decomposers break compost down, the compost heap becomes warmer due to the respiration of the bacteria and fungi generating heat. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, only two of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Plant Description
I. - Asafoetida Fleshy roots
II. - Bitter Gourd Tropical vine
III. - Coriander Perennial Herb
IV. - Turmeric Underground rhizome
V. - Banana Woody trunk
How many of the above pairs are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Asafoetida** (Ferula asafetida)**:** It is **herbaceous, monoecious, perennial herb** that belongs to the family Apiaceae (Umbelliferaceae). The plant has a fetid smell due to the presence of sulfur constituents. Asafoetida (vern. Hing) is the dried latex or oleo gum resin extracted from the **fleshy roots** of this perennial herb. Its roots are massive, thick and pulpy. The outer bark of the root is blackish and wrinkled while it is fleshy, white, containing thick milky, fetid alliaceous juice from the inner surface. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Bitter gourd (****Momordica charantia****):** It is a **tropical vine** belonging to the order Cucurbitales, family Cucubitaceae and genus Momordica.  The plant possesses **characteristic leaves with serrate margins** which typically give a look like bites. Each plant has separate yellow coloured male and female flowers. In general, the plant is a **monoecious slender, tendril climbing annual vine** of almost 2 to 4 m high. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched.**
● **Coriander (****Coriandrum sativum****):** It is an **annual spice crop** and a member of the carrot family. It has an erect, branching stem growing to a height of approximately 0.6 to one m (two to three ft.). It is an **annual** **herb**, meaning it completes its life cycle within one year. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Turmeric (****Curcuma longa)****:** It is a **perennial herbaceous plant** and a member of the ginger family (Zingiberaceae), which is known for its **tubers (rhizomes) below the ground.**  Turmeric is sterile (does not produce seed, but it does grow vigorously from the rhizomes)**.** The **rhizome (underground stem) is thick** **and ringed** with the bases of old leaves. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.**
● **Banana (****Musa****):** It is a **tree-like perennial herb**. It is an herb because it does not have woody tissues and the fruit-bearing stem dies down after the growing season. **What looks like a trunk is not a woody stem but** **a pseudostem, a compact assemblage of overlapping and spirally arranged leaf sheaths**. The 'true' stem is made up of three parts: the underground rhizome, the aerial stem to which are attached the leaves, and the peduncle to which is attached the inflorescence. **So, pair V is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
What is common between Prosopis juliflora and
Water hyacinth?

options.b:
Both are known for their aromatic medicinal
properties
options.c:
Both have woody stems and serve as a source
of fuelwood

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Prosopis Juliflora:** It is a **thorny invasive tree species** introduced in India, particularly in arid and semiarid regions, to combat desertification. It is considered **India's most hated tree** due to its aggressive spread, displacement of native flora, depletion of groundwater, and its near-impossibility to eradicate. It transforms fertile lands into barren patches.
o It is native to **Mexico, South America and the Caribbean**.
o It has a **very wide ecological adaptability** which can grow on soils from sand dune to clay soil, and from saline to alkaline soil type.
● **Water Hyacinth:** Water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes) are invasive species that often appears as a green layer of dense vegetation on rivers, ponds, and lakes. A fast-growing aquatic plant, it normally provides shelter and feeding grounds for a range of aquatic animals. However, under conditions where water bodies are polluted with a high nutrient load, these free-floating and highly invasive aquatic plants can proliferate rapidly, forming a dense green layer on water bodies and posing a risk to the natural habitats of the freshwater ecosystems.
o **Water hyacinth is native to the tropical regions of South America**, originating from the
Amazon basin and spreading naturally throughout the continent. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, Biosphere Reserve
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regards
to the Conservation Reserves and Community
Reserves:
I. 	 Conservation reserves and community
reserves in India are buffer zones around
national parks.
II. 	 These protected area categories were first
introduced in the Environment Protection Act
of 1986.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Conservation reserves and community reserves** in India are terms denoting protected areas of India
which typically act as **buffer zones,connectors, and migration corridors** between established **national** **parks, wildlife sanctuaries** and reserved and protected forests in India. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Such areas are designated as conservation reserves if they are uninhabited and completely owned by the Government of India but used for subsistence by communities, and community reserves if part of the land is privately owned.
● **These protected area categories were first introduced in the Wildlife (Protection) Amendment Act** **of 2002, an amendment to the Wildlife Protection Act (WLPA) of 1972. So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● These categories were added because of reduced protection in and around existing or proposed protected areas due to private ownership of land, and land use.
● The provisions of the WLPA apply to an area once it has been declared a community reserve. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Hangul:
I. 	 It is the only species of red deer found in the
Indian subcontinent.
II. 	 It is listed as ‘Critically Endangered’ on the
IUCN Red List.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kashmir stag** is also called **Hangul** which is a subspecies of **Central Asian red deer endemic to Kashmir** and surrounding areas. It is found in dense riverine forests in the high valleys and mountains of Jammu and Kashmir and northern Himachal Pradesh. In Kashmir, it is found primarily in **the Dachigam National** **Park** where it receives protection.
● It is the **only species of red deer** in the Indian subcontinent. Red deers are ungulates belonging to the subfamily Cervinae, they are characterized by their elaborated antlers and red coat. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Hangul is classified as **Critically Endangered** on the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Red List and is also listed in **Appendix I of CITES**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**Dachigam National Park:**
● The actual beauty of the park lies in the **deep valleys, rocky outcrops, steep wooded** slopes and rolling alpine pastures.
● Being located in a mountainous area, Dachigam National Park faces a huge variation in **altitude** that ranges from **1600 m to 4200 m above sea level.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following processes:
I. 	 Natality
II. 	 Mortality
III. 	Immigration
IV. 	 Emigration
How many of the above contributes towards an
increase in population density of a species?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Population density** is the concentration of individuals within a species in a specific geographic location.
● It is not a static parameter and is dependent on various factors including food availability, predation pressure and adverse weather. These factors contribute to the dynamics of population density,
which changes due to fluctuation in four basic processes – **Immigration, Natality, Emigration** **and Mortality**, the first two contributes towards increasing the density, while the last two decrease the population density.
o **Natality (Y)**: It refers to the number of births during a given period in the population that are added to the initial density. **So, point I is correct.** o **Mortality:** It is the number of deaths in the population during a given period. **So, point II is not** **correct.** o **Immigration(X):** It is the number of individuals of the same species that have come into the habitat from elsewhere during the time period under consideration. **So, point III is correct.** o **Emigration(Z):** It is the number of individuals of the population who left the habitat and gone elsewhere during the time period under consideration. **So, point II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following is most likely to have
the highest Biological Oxygen Demand?

options.a:
Running water streams across a hydropower
project
options.b: Water pond near an agricultural field
options.c:
Wetland continuously being fed by a river
stream

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is the amount of oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to decompose the organic matter present in a given volume of water at a certain temperature over a specified period of time.  It serves as an **indicator of organic pollution**, with higher BOD values reflecting greater contamination and lower water quality
● **Water ponds near agricultural fields** often receive runoff containing fertilizers, pesticides, manure, and organic residues. These inputs are rich in nitrogen and phosphorus, which act as nutrients for microbial growth. This results in an **elevated microbial population**. Consequently, BOD levels are **highest** here due to intense oxygen demand.
o Studies show that small water bodies near agricultural zones often exhibit BOD values between **10–50 mg/L or higher**, which is much greater than flowing rivers (1–2 mg/L) or estuaries (2–8 mg/L). This empirical evidence confirms such ponds are **hotspots for oxygen depletion** due to high BOD. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Running water streams across a hydropower project** have fast-moving water, which promotes natural aeration and rapid dilution of organic matter. As a result, there is little accumulation of biodegradable material and minimal microbial oxygen demand. Thus, BOD remains low in such water bodies.
● **Wetlands** have some organic matter from plant litter and river inflow, supporting moderate microbial decomposition. However, their water exchange and filtration capacity prevent
excessive organic accumulation. Therefore, BOD is **moderate**, higher than running water but lower than stagnant ponds.
● **Estuaries** experience tidal mixing and regular inflow of fresh and saline water, which helps dilute organic pollutants. Though some organic matter comes from rivers and nearby human activities, oxygen depletion is not severe. Hence, BOD is **low to moderate** in estuarine waters.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
National Park State
I. - Clouded Leopard National Park
Tripura
II. - Blackbuck National Park
Gujarat
III. - Bison National Park
Odisha
IV. - Eravikulam National Park
Tamil Nadu
How many of the above pairs are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Clouded Leopard National** **Park:** It is located within the Sepahijala Wildlife Sanctuary in **Tripura.** It is India's first dedicated wildlife park for clouded leopards. The sanctuary, which houses the park, is also home to various primate monkeys, including Phayre's langur. **So, pair I is** **correctly matched.**
● **Blackbuck National Park:** It is also known as Velavadar National Park, is located in the Bhavnagar district of **Gujarat.** Blackbuck, wolves, hyenas, wild boar, jackals, lesser floricans, and various bird species are some commonly found species here. **So, Pair II** **is correctly matched**
● **Bison National Park:** It is also known as Rajbari National Park, is located in the Trishna Wildlife Sanctuary in **Tripura,** **India**. It was established to protect the Indian gaur, also known as bison. **So, Pair III is not** **correctly matched**
● **Eravikulam National Park:** It is situated in the Kannan Devan Hills of the southern **Western Ghats of** **Kerala.** This is also the land of “Neelakurinji”, the flower that blooms once in twelve years. The Anamudi peak is situated on the southern side of the Park. The Nilgiri Tahr, Gaur, Sloth Bear, Nilgiri Langur, Tiger, Leopard, Giant Squirrel and wild dog are commonly found species here. **So, Pair IV is not correctly** **matched.** **So, only two of the above given pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Dugong:
I. 	 Dugongs inhabit shallow coastal waters of
the Indo-Pacific region.
II. 	 They serve as indicators of seagrass
ecosystem health.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **dugong** (Dugong dugon) is a large, gentle marine mammal often referred to as a **"sea cow"**. The dugong is the only surviving species of the family Dugongidae and is closely related to manatees.
● Dugongs inhabit **shallow coastal waters** of the Indo-Pacific region from East Africa and the Red Sea to northern Australia and the western Pacific. It is known for its peaceful, slow-moving nature and **herbivorous diet.** **So, statement I is correct.**
o Dugongs prefer **seagrass forest areas** because these shallow coastal habitats provide their **only** **food source**, seagrass.
● These creatures **have a fusiform (streamlined) body, a fluke tail like dolphins, and bristled,** **downturned snouts** adapted for uprooting seagrass. This helps maintain healthy seagrass meadows,
which support broader coastal biodiversity and also serve as **indicators of ecosystem health.** This **feeding** **behaviour classifies them as benthic feeders (bottom feeders).** Their feeding leaves visible trails in the seagrass beds, which researchers use to monitor their presence and habitat use. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
This marine turtle species, known for its large
migratory and diving habits, lacks the peculiar
hard shell as seen in other marine turtles. It also
doesn't have crushing beaks like in other turtles,
thus making it dependent on soft bodied gelatinous
prey.
Which one of the following is best described in
the above paragraph?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● **Turtles belong to one of the oldest reptile groups in the world.** Turtles are easily recognised by their bony, cartilaginous shell. This super-tough casing acts like a shield to protect them from predators – some turtles can even tuck their head up inside their shell for extra protection. A turtle’s shell is part of its skeleton. It’s made up of over 50 bones which include the turtle’s rib cage and spine.
● **The leatherback sea turtle is the largest turtle in the world**. They are the **only species of sea turtle** **that lack scales and a hard shell.** They are named for their tough rubbery skin and have existed in their current form since the age of the dinosaurs.
● Leatherbacks are **highly migratory,** some swimming over 10,000 miles a year between nesting and foraging grounds. They are also accomplished **divers with the deepest recorded dive reaching nearly** **4,000 feet**—deeper than most marine mammals. **The leatherback turtle has the widest global** **distribution** of any reptile, with nesting mainly on tropical or subtropical beaches.
● The turtles lack teeth, their diet therefore consists of soft small organisms, but in the loggerhead turtle jaws are scissor-like, adapted for gripping and slicing soft prey, rather than crushing hard shells as in other turtle species. Their diet primarily consists of jellyfish and other gelatinous organisms.
● They are classified as **Endangered** on the IUCN Redlist and protected under Appendix I of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES). **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Olive Ridley Turtle** has a hard, bony shell and feeds on a variety of prey. This species is among the smallest, but the most widespread and abundant of the world’s sea turtles. Olive ridleys have two nesting strategies, solitary or mass nesting (arribada). Olive Ridleys are omnivores and exhibit arribada, a mass nesting event where thousands of female nest together. Gahirmatha and Rushikulya in Odisha are among the world’s largest nesting sites for Olive Ridley turtles. They are Vulnerable as per the IUCN Redlist and protected under Appendix I of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES).
● The **loggerhead turtle** is named for its large head, which supports powerful jaw muscles that enable them to feed on hard-shelled prey, such as whelks and conch. The top shell
(carapace) is slightly heart-shaped and reddish-brown in adults and sub-adults, while the bottom shell (plastron) is generally a pale yellowish colour. Adult female sea turtles return to land to lay their eggs on sandy beaches—they are remarkable navigators and usually return to a beach in the general area where they hatched decades earlier. They are Vulnerable as per the IUCN Redlist.
● **The Indian Softshell turtle** (**Nilssonia gangetica**), also known as the Ganges Softshell turtle, is a reptile found in freshwater habitats, and its distribution is restricted to the Ganges, Indus and Mahanadi rivers in northern and eastern India. Some of the states in
which they are found include Assam, Bihar, Jammu & Kashmir, Madhya Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Orissa, Rajasthan and West Bengal. This turtle can be easily distinguished by its prominent, tube-like snout and flattened shell. Its external features allow it to perfectly adapt to a riparian habitat (domain around rivers and other water bodies). Their long necks and tube-like snouts help them to extend their nose out of the water to breathe. The compressed shell, on the other hand, streamlines them, thereby making them brilliant and fast swimmers.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Asiatic Elephant, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 They have the largest gestation period
amongst mammals.
II. 	 They have characteristic incisor teeth which
protrude out of their mouth.
III. 	Their trunk has two finger-like projections at
the end, which act like fingers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● **Elephants are the largest land mammals in the world**, so it’s perhaps not surprising that they have the longest pregnancy of any living mammal: African elephants are pregnant for an average of 22 months, whilst for Asian elephants it’s 18 to 22 months. Thus, **it is the African elephant which has the longest** **gestation period and not Asian Elephants**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Elephants usually have 26 teeth: the incisors, known as the tusks, 12 deciduous premolars, and 12 molars. Unlike most mammals, which grow baby teeth and then replace them with a single permanent set of adult teeth, elephants have cycles of tooth rotation throughout their lives. The chewing teeth are replaced six times in a typical elephant's lifetime. The characteristic teeth that protrude out of an elephant's mouth are tusks, which are modified incisor teeth. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **"snout" refers to the trunk.** A key distinguishing feature of the Asian elephant's trunk tip is that it has only one finger-like projection. **African elephants have two dorsal fingers to their trunk which** **acts like two fingers** and works in a pinching potion whereas the Asian elephant has only one dorsal finger to their trunk and will curl their trunk around an object to grasp it. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● Asian elephants have three main subspecies- Sumatran (the smallest and palest), Indian (in the middle) and Sri Lankan (usually the biggest and darkest). The world’s largest elephant on record was an Indian elephant – he weighed 7.7 tons and stood 3.43m tall! Elephants typically weigh up to 6.5 tons in Africa and 5.5 tons in Asia. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Ramsar Site State/Union Territory
I. - Tso Kar Jammu and Kashmir
II. - Pala Mizoram
III. - Tampara Odisha
IV. - Wadhwana Tripura
V. - Rudrasagar Gujarat
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Convention on Wetlands** is the intergovernmental treaty that provides the framework for the conservation and wise use of wetlands and their resources. The Convention was adopted in the Iranian city of Ramsar in 1971 and came into force in 1975. Since then, almost 90% of UN member states, from all the world’s geographic regions, have acceded to become “Contracting Parties”.
● **Tso Kar lake** is in the Union Territory of **Ladakh**. It consists of two principal water bodies: Startsapuk Tso, a freshwater lake, Tso Kar itself, situated in the Changthang region of Ladakh. It is an A1 Category
Important Bird Area (IBA) as per Bird Life International. It is the most important breeding area of the near threatened, Black-necked Crane (**Grus nigricollis**) in India. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Pala Wetland is a Ramsar site located in Mizoram.** Pala Wetland is the largest natural wetland in the state of Mizoram. Pala Wetland is revered by the local Mara people and has a deep connection with their history. The wetland is the major source of freshwater and fish for the villages at its fringe and also the major source of irrigation water. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Tampara Lake is a Ramsar site located in Odisha.** It is an important habitat for vulnerable species such as Cyprinus carpio, common pochard (Aythya ferina), and river tern (Sterna aurantia). **So, pair III is** **correctly matched.**
● **Wadhwana Wetland is a Ramsar site located in Gujarat.** The wetland is internationally important for its birdlife as it provides wintering ground to migratory waterbirds. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
● **Rudrasagar Lake is a Ramsar site located in Tripura.** It is a lowland sedimentation reservoir in the northeast hills, fed by three perennial streams discharging to the River Gomti. Owing to high rainfall (2500mm) and downstream topography, the wetland is regularly flooded with 4-5 times annual peak, assisting in groundwater recharge. **So, pair V is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
Which of the following conditions will favour the
exponential population growth for a species?
I. 	 Constant environmental conditions
II. 	 High population density
III. 	Absence of predation
IV. 	 Limited resources
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● The size of a population for any species is not a static parameter. It keeps changing with time, depending on various factors including food availability, predation pressure and adverse weather.  **The simplest type** **of population growth is exponential growth.**
● Exponential growth **occurs when a single species is not limited by other species (no predation,** **parasitism, competitors), resources are not limited, and the environment is constant.** These conditions are called **an ‘ecological vacuum’,** and this does not often occur (for long) in nature. However, in nature, a given habitat has enough resources to support a maximum possible number, beyond which no further growth is possible. **This limit is known as nature’s carrying capacity (K)** for that species in that habitat.
o A population growing in a habitat with limited resources shows initially a lag phase, followed by phases of acceleration and deceleration and finally an asymptote, when the population density reaches the carrying capacity. This type of population growth is called **Logistic Growth.**
● The **constant environmental conditions** provide optimal conditions for reproduction and survival without population fluctuations or stress. Therefore, stable environmental conditions will **promote** **exponential population growth.**  **So, point I is correct.**
● **High population density** leads to factors like, increased competition for resources and higher chances of predation. These factors are **density-dependent limiting factors** **that** **inhibit exponential growth** and push the population towards logistic growth. **Low density favors** **the exponential growth of a** **population** because it causes less or no competition and ample availability of resources which leads to exponential rise in population. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Predation** is an extrinsic factor that **increases the death rate and decreases the population size**. For a population to grow exponentially, there should be minimal or no predation. **Absence of predation** **will** **facilitate the exponential growth** of a population by reducing the death rate due to external factors whereas birth rate of the population will remain unaffected and with time population will grow exponentially. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Resources,** like food and space, availability is essential for the unimpeded growth of a population. When resources in the habitat are unlimited, each member of a population can realise fully its innate potential to
grow in number. Then the population grows exponentially. **But when resources are limited,** a **population will not grow exponentially** **due to the competition** among members of the same population.  **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Ecology and Ecosystem
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** House sparrows are witnessing sharp population declines in urban areas.
**Statement II:** House sparrows are exclusively grain eating birds. **Statement III:** House sparrows prefer open grasslands for foraging.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct
but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The House Sparrow is native to all the Indian States. A non-migratory species, **house sparrows are** **closely tied to human activity**. This sparrow is usually absent from extensive woodlands and forests and from grasslands and deserts. House Sparrows are typically present only in the vicinity of human habitation.
● **House sparrows**, once a common sight in urban areas, are now witnessing sharp population declines. This is primarily due **to increased competition from urban-adapted species like Rock Pigeons, Mynas,** **and House Crows**. These birds are more flexible in their diet, nesting habits, and can better exploit the altered urban ecology. In contrast, sparrows require specific conditions for feeding and nesting, which are rapidly disappearing in built-up urban spaces. **So, statement I is correct.**
● While house sparrows do consume grains, they are not exclusively grain-eating. **A large part of their** **diet includes burrowing insects and worms, especially during the breeding season when chicks need** **protein-rich food to survive**. This dependence on insects makes them sensitive to declines in insect populations, which are often caused by habitat loss and pesticide use in urban areas. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● **House sparrows tend to forage in areas that provide insect life and seeds, typically found in weedy** **patches, open grasslands and gardens**. In highly urbanized zones with manicured lawns or cemented areas, these natural foraging grounds are absent, leading to scarcity of food. Sparrows are often recorded in urban villages or green spaces like parks and wetlands, where such vegetation is still present. Therefore, their preference for these rough, grassy foraging zones is an important factor in understanding their population dynamics. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **House sparrow is considered as an indicator species for a healthy urban ecosystem.** It is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Ex-Situ and In-Situ Conservation
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Biosphere Reserves
II. 	 National Parks
III. 	 Wildlife Sanctuaries
IV. 	 Sacred Groves
V. 	 Botanical gardens
How many of the above are in situ conservation methods for biological resources?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **In-situ conservation** refers to the protection of **genetic resources** within their **natural habitats**. It involves safeguarding **endangered** **plants** **and** **animals** in the ecosystems where they naturally occur, either by protecting and restoring **habitats** or defending species from **predators**. This method is also applied to **agricultural** **biodiversity** through **agroforestry** and traditional **farming practices**. **So, point V is correct.**
● In India, such conservation is achieved by designating areas as **protected zones**, which include **national** **parks**, **wildlife sanctuaries**, and **biosphere reserves**. **So, points I, II and III are correct.**
○ **National parks** are strictly reserved for **wildlife**, where activities like **grazing, cultivation, and** **forestry** are prohibited, with boundaries clearly demarcated and a focus on preserving **single** **species** in relatively smaller areas. ○ **Wildlife sanctuaries** are protected zones mainly for **animals**, where limited **human activity** such as collecting **forest produce** or **tourism** is permitted as long as it does not interfere with wildlife. ○ **Biosphere reserves** are large areas, often **over 5000 sq. km**, where **humans coexist** as part of the system. They consist of a **core zone** that is strictly protected, a **buffer zone** that permits **research** **and education**, and a **transition zone** that allows **activities like agriculture and forestry**.
■ Biosphere reserves perform three major functions: **conservation** of species and ecosystems, **development** that sustains cultural and ecological identity, and **scientific** **research** for testing integrated management practices. They serve as **living laboratories** for sustainable development.
● **Sacred groves** can be classified under the purview of in situ conservation of biodiversity. **So, point IV is** **correct.**
○ These are patches of lush greenery protected and preserved on religious and cultural grounds. ○ The ancient Indian society tried to create a synergy of religion and nature to protect wilderness with a wide range of rare herbs, shrubs, reptiles, medicinal plants, ancient trees and so on. ○ They are often associated with the local deity linked to an ancient, prehistoric tradition and customs in an area.
● **Botanical gardens** maintain collections of plants, including exotic species, for education and research. It is an ex situ conservation method of biological resources. **So, point V is not correct.** **So, only four of the above are in situ conservation methods for biological resources.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **What are other methods of conservation?**
● **Ex-situ conservation**, on the other hand, is the preservation of **biodiversity** outside its **natural** **habitats**.
● It includes a wide range of techniques and facilities such as **seed gene banks**, **gene banks**, **cryopreservation**, **tissue culture**, **captive breeding**, **botanical gardens**, **translocation**, and **zoological gardens**.
● **Seed gene banks** store plant seeds at controlled temperature and humidity, ensuring long-term viability.
● **Gene banks** maintain genetic material under controlled conditions, while cryopreservation suspends biological activity at –196°C in liquid nitrogen. Tissue culture banks preserve plant parts like meristems, ensuring disease-free propagation.
● **Captive breeding** is used for endangered species when natural habitats are lost or unsuitable.
● **Translocation** involves moving animals to new habitats when existing conditions become unfavorable or populations become unsustainable.
● **Zoological gardens**, such as the world’s oldest **Schonbrunn Zoo in Vienna (1759)** and India’s first zoo at **Barrackpore (1800)**, play a vital role in maintaining and breeding **endangered** **wildlife**.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following ecological interactions:
I. 	 Mutualism
II. 	 Ammensalism
III. 	Competition
IV. 	 Parasitism
V. 	 Commensalism In how many of the above, none of the interacting organisms gets benefitted?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● In any natural ecosystem, organisms interact in various ways forming a **biological community** in such a way that **no species exists in** **isolation**. These interactions can be **beneficial** **(+), detrimental (–) or neutral (0)** to each other.
● When two species interact, the **effect on each** **species** is used to classify the interaction. Some of the different types of interactions include,
● **Mutualism** **confers benefits on both the interacting species**. For example: Lichens represent an intimate mutualistic relationship between a fungus and photosynthesising algae or cyanobacteria. Similarly, the mycorrhizae are associations between fungi and the roots of higher plants. The fungi help the plant in the absorption of essential nutrients from the soil while the plant in turn provides the fungi with energyyielding carbohydrates. Thus, in mutualism both the organisms are benefited. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **In amensalism one species is harmed whereas the other is unaffected.** For example: Black Walnut trees produce a chemical called juglone (a naphthoquinone) that is highly toxic to many other plant species. **Juglone inhibits** the growth, respiration, and enzyme activity of sensitive plants, causing them to wilt, stunt, or even die. This creates a "toxic zone" around the walnut tree where other plants struggle to survive. The black walnut tree itself is not affected by the presence or absence of these sensitive plants, nor does it derive a direct benefit from their harm beyond potentially reducing competition for resources indirectly. **Therefore, in case of amensalism none of the interacting species got benefitted. So, point** **II is correct.**
● **Competition is the type of interaction where both the species are at a loss**. Totally **unrelated species** **may compete for the same resource** e.g. in shallow creeks on the west coast of Mumbai, visiting flamingos and resident fish compete for their common food, the zooplankton. Secondly, **resources need** **not always be limiting for competition to occur.** In competition, the feeding efficiency of one species is reduced due to the interference or inhibitory presence of the other species, even if resources (food and space) are abundant, e.g. Leopards do not hunt near lion pride. Competition is best defined as a process in
which the fitness of one species is significantly lower in the presence of another species. **Therefore, in** **the case of competition none of the interacting species got benefitted. So, point III is correct.**
● **In parasitism** **only one species benefits (parasite) and the interaction is detrimental to the other** **species**. Parasitism has evolved in so many taxonomic groups from plants to higher vertebrates. Many parasites have evolved to be host-specific (they can parasitize only a single species of host) in such a way that both host and the parasite tend to co-evolve, against each other. **Most of the parasites harm the host**; they may reduce the survival, growth and reproduction of the host and reduce its population density. They might render the host more vulnerable to predation by making it physically weak. **Therefore, in case of** **parasitism one of the interacting species got benefitted. So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Commensalism** is the type of interaction in which **one species benefits and the other is neither harmed** **nor benefited. For example**: An orchid growing as an epiphyte on a mango branch, and barnacles growing on the back of a whale benefit while neither the mango tree nor the whale derives any apparent benefit. **Therefore, in case of commensalism one of the interacting species got benefitted. So, point** **V is not correct.** **So, in only two of the above, none of the interacting organisms got benefitted.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Dicloflenac and Nimesulide are a major cause for declining vulture population in India.
**Statement II:** Dicloflenac and Nimesulide are nephrotoxic drugs known to spike uric acid levels in the body. **Statement III:** Dicloflenac and Nimesulide have shorter half-life compared to other anti-inflammatory drugs.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct
but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vultures are recognized as the scroungers of the natural world**, owing to their ecological role as obligate scavengers. India’s vulture populations, once numbering in the tens of millions, have suffered catastrophic declines in recent decades due to the rampant use of veterinary drugs, primarily **Non-** **Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs) such as Dicloflenac, Nimuselide, Ketorolac etc.**
● These drugs **used to treat inflammatory ailments in cattle are known to leave residue in the animal** **bodies**, improper disposal of their carcasses to be fed on for scavengers have led to dwindling numbers of vultures in the country. **So, statement I is correct.**
● These drugs are **nephrotoxic in almost all animals as they are known to spike uric acid** levels in the body. While the large animals like humans and cattle may tolerate this spike, vultures are not biologically equipped to handle such elevated levels of uric acid, resulting in kidney failure and ultimately death. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● Despite a ban on NSAIDs like Dicloflenac (banned in India for veterinary preparation since 2006) and Nimuselide (banned recently in December 2024) **these drugs are still being used because of their short** **half-lives,** making them more efficient than other alternatives present in the market. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Red Sanders:
I. 	 It is endemic to the Western Ghats.
II. 	 Its trade is completely prohibited under
CITES.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Red Sanders**, also known as red sandalwood, is a rare and economically valuable tree species endemic to southern India. It has a deep red heartwood prized for its use in musical instruments, traditional medicine, and luxury woodcraft, particularly in East Asian markets.
● **Red Sanders are endemic to a specific region in the Eastern Ghats**, particularly within Andhra Pradesh, across districts like Chittoor, Kadapa, Nandyal, and Nellore. The species’ natural range is highly restricted and fragmented, covering less than 6 sq km in actual occupancy. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Red Sanders was reclassified as ‘**Endangered’** on the IUCN Red List in 2021. This shift came after being placed under the 'Near Threatened' category in 2018. The downgrade reflects a sharp population decline, estimated between 50% and 80% over three generations primarily driven by overharvesting and illegal trade.
● **Red Sanders is listed under Appendix II of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered** **Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES).** This means that while trade is not entirely banned, it is **strictly regulated and requires permits** to ensure it does not threaten the species’ survival in the wild. Despite this protection, illegal international trade persists. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Red Sanders faces major threats from illegal logging, driven by high international demand, particularly from China and Japan. Compounding this, the species has slow natural regeneration, making it vulnerable to population collapse. Additional pressures include grazing, invasive species, and habitat fragmentation, leaving only a few mature individuals in the wild. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following Biosphere reserves of
India:
I. 	 Panna
II. 	 Dehang-Debang
III.	 Khangchendzonga
IV. 	 Rann of Kutch
V. 	 Manas
How many of the above are included under the Man and Biosphere programme of UNESCO?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Man and the Biosphere Programme (MAB)** is a major international initiative launched by **UNESCO** in 1971 to promote a balanced relationship between humans and nature with following objectives.
o **In-situ** **Conservation:** To conserve biodiversity within the natural habitat, including genes, species, and ecosystems as a whole. o **Scientific** **Understanding:** To promote research, education, and monitoring to better understand how ecosystems function and interact. o **Integrated** **Development:** To ensure sustainable development by improving the quality of life of local and indigenous communities living in and around the reserve.
● India joined the MAB Programme in **1971**. As of 2025, **12 out of 18 Indian Biosphere Reserves** are included in the World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) under UNESCO-MAB.
● **The 12 biosphere reserves include:** o Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve, Karnataka o Gulf of Mannar Biosphere Reserve, Tamil Nadu o Sunderban Biosphere Reserve, West Bengal o **Panna** Biosphere Reserve, Madhya Pradesh. **So, point I is correct.** o Nanda Devi Biosphere Reserve, Uttrakhand o Nokrek Biosphere Reserve, Meghalaya o Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve, Madhya Pradesh o Similipal Biosphere Reserve, Odhisha o Achanakmar-Amarkantak Biosphere Reserve, Madhya Pradesh o Great Nicobar Biosphere Reserve, Andaman and Nicobar Islands (UT) o Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve, Kerala and Tamil Nadu o **Khangchendzonga** Biosphere Reserve**,** Sikkim. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **Dehang Debang Biosphere Reserve** in Arunachal Pradesh, **Rann of Kutch** in Gujarat and **Manas** in Assam are not part of the MAB. **So, points II, IV and V are not correct** **So, only two of the above are included under the Man and Biosphere programme of UNESCO.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Both National Parks and Wildlife Sanctuaries
have well defined boundaries delineated via the Gazette notification by the Government.
II. 	 The approval from the National Board of
Wildlife is mandatory for changing the boundaries of a National Park.
III. 	Once an area is notified as a Wildlife
Sanctuary the same cannot be de-notified by the State Government.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **National Parks and Wildlife Sanctuaries** are protected areas of high ecological, floral, faunal, geomorphological, or natural significance. They are **notified by State Governments** and managed primarily by the State Forest Departments under the **Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.**
● **Boundaries of Wildlife Sanctuaries are not well defined** and controlled biotic interference (such as controlled human use) is permitted. National Parks have **clearly defined and fixed boundaries** notified
in the Official Gazette, and no biotic interference (e.g., resource extraction, grazing) is permitted. **So,** **Statement I is not correct.**
● In **National Parks**, all rights of local communities must be **completely settled before declaration**, and **no rights of ownership or use** are allowed within the park. In the **Wildlife Sanctuaries**, certain **traditional or permitted rights** (such as grazing, forest produce collection) may continue if they do not interfere with wildlife conservation.
● As per the provisions of the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972, **no alteration of the boundaries of a** **sanctuary or National Park can be made by the State Governments except on a recommendation of** **the National Board for Wild Life (NBWL).** Besides, no alteration in the boundaries of a tiger reserve can be made except on the recommendation of the National Tiger Conservation Authority and the approval of the National Board for Wild Life. **So, statement II is correct.**
● A **State Government can de-notify any of the Wildlife Sanctuary** in its jurisdiction, but for it also an approval from the NBWL is required. **So, statement III is not correct** **So, only one of the above given statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, Biosphere Reserve
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Community Reserves in India:
I. 	 Any region within a Wildlife Sanctuary or
National Park can be declared as a Community
Reserve.
II. 	 The Chief Wildlife Warden of the State is
the nodal agency for the conservation and
management of the Community Reserves.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Conservation reserves and community reserves** are protected areas of India which typically act as buffer zones between established **national parks, wildlife sanctuaries and reserved and protected** **forests** of India. These protected area categories were first introduced in the Wild Life (Protection) Amendment Act of 2002 [an amendment to the parent act Wildlife Protection Act, 1972].
● A **Community Reserve** is a category of protected area established and managed by **local communities** **in collaboration with the State Government**. These reserves aim to conserve wildlife and biodiversity while also promoting the socio-economic well-being of local populations.
● **No region within a Wildlife Sanctuary or National Park** can be declared as a Community Reserve. Community Reserves are specifically designated on private or community land that is not part of existing National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, or Conservation Reserves. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The State Government may notify any private or community-owned land as a Community Reserve, with the consent of the concerned individuals or community members. Such areas are intended to protect not only flora and fauna, but also the traditional knowledge, cultural practices, and lifestyles of the communities.
● Once declared, **the management of such reserves is vested in a Community Reserve Management** **Committee**, which shall be the authority responsible for conserving, maintaining and managing the community reserve although there is overall supervision, regulation, and technical supervision by the Chief Wildlife Warden. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Tiger and Lion:
I. 	 Both belong to the same genus taxonomically.
II. 	 Both occupy the same niche ecologically.
III. 	Both are solitary organisms.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Tiger and Lion are the top predators in their respective environments, both are big cat species of the same **genus Panthera**, with tigers belonging to the tigris species (Panthera tigris) and lions belonging to the Leo species (Panthera leo). **So, statement I is correct.**
● Lion and Tiger share **different ecological niches,** with Lions being the top predators of grassland ecosystem and Tigers being the same for dense forests, this distinction provides for their possible coexistence. For example, Asiatic lions and Bengal tigers have co-existed in the Indian subcontinent
across parts of India, Central Asia, and Iran for millennia largely attributed by the **different niches, social** **contrast and different habitats. So, statement II is not correct.**
o **Lions** (Panthera leo) prefer **open, dry, savannah and grassland habitats**, typical of **Afrotropical zones**. Example: Gir Forest in India is a **dry deciduous scrub** aligned with lion preferences. o **Tigers** (Panthera tigris): Thrive in **dense forests, mangroves, tropical and subtropical moist** **habitats**, typical of the **Indo-Malayan realm**. They are found in **Sundarbans**, Himalayan foothills, and tropical evergreen forests.
● **Lions live in groups called prides,** which provide a defensive advantage and support cooperative hunting strategies. Their **social nature makes them well-adapted to open habitats,** where group hunting is more effective. In contrast, **tigers are solitary by nature** and typically avoid confrontations with groups. As ambush predators, tigers prefer dense vegetation that offers cover for stalking and capturing prey. **So,** **statement III is not correct.** **So, only one of the above given statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Aspects** **Lion** **Tiger**
**Scientific Name** Panthera leo Panthera tigris
**Social Structure** Social – lives in prides Solitary
**Habitat** Grasslands, savannas Dense forests, mangroves
**Average Weight (Male)** 150–250 kg 180–300+ kg
**Length (With Tail)**
2.4–3 m
2.7–3.3 m
**Bite Force** 650–1,000 psi ~1,050 psi
**Speed** Up to 80 km/h (in short bursts) Up to 65 km/h (short bursts),
more agile
**Combat Strategy** Uses group support; defensive in solo
fights
Uses stealth and power; more
aggressive in solo fights
**Fighting Experience** More experience fighting other males
(within pride)
Hunts and fights alone – more
direct combat experience
**Adaptability** Adapted to open areas and teamwork Adapted to dense cover and solo
ambush
**Strength (muscularity)** Strong, but slightly less muscular than
tiger
Highly muscular; built for
explosive strength
**Outcome in 1v1 Fight** Likely to lose against a tiger Likely to win due to superior size,
strength, agility
**Points to remember**
● The Panthera lineage consists of **seven cats**, five of which are the **largest** in the cat family (Felidae) and belong to Panthera genus while the two Clouded Leopards belong to the Neofelis genus.
●  The five largest cats of the Panthera lineage include **lion, tiger, leopard, jaguar, and snow leopard.**
● **Characteristics of the** **Panthera** **genus:** o Large, muscular body with a large skull, long tail and broad paws. o The skull structure and size is a key feature that differs for each of the Felidae genera. o Males are heavier and larger than females. o The first four big cats in the Panthera genus are the **only wild cats that have the ability to** **roar**, however they cannot purr like most other cats. o The fifth cat, the Snow Leopard, is the big cat exception in this genus that cannot roar.
